observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exert extra efforts to conduct verification, analysis and validation of these receivable balances. We also recommended that Management submit again a request letter to write-off the receivable accounts that are dormant and uncollectible, following the procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005, and supported by the documents enumerated in Section 8.3 of the same circular.
observations.managementAction: Full disclosure was done in the financial statements in order to inform the users that these accounts are already dormant.
observations.reason: Difficulty in securing documents to support request for write-off.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management prioritize the preparation/processing of documentary requirements to secure the TCT and other supporting documents of the purchased lots to support the claims of their ownership. The lands recorded in the books which were not supported with TCT and other legal documents of ownership must be properly disclosed in the notes to financial statements.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the documentary requirements to support the payments of the purchased lots as prescribed under PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant stated that strict compliance with the proper documentation of transactions will be implemented prior to the approval of disbursement vouchers.

Management promised to allocate funds for the titling/transfer of the lots. The LGU will delegate personnel to focus and prioritize the preparation and submission of the supporting documents to support claims of ownership.
observations.reason:
No fund allocation and lack of personnel who will process the land titling.

Same reason in number 39.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and all the members of the LSB ensure that the PPAs for ECCD be incorporated in the budget for SEF. We also recommended that Management widen the scope of the SEF budget and prioritize the PPAs with long term effects/involvement on public schools, teachers, students and other stakeholders. Additionally, the provisions of DepEd-DBM-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2017 in the utilization of the SEF for its intended purpose must be strictly observed.
observations.managementAction: Management promised that in the next SEF Budget preparation, the LSB will be prioritizing the PPAs enumerated in Item 4.0 of DepEd-DBM-DILG JMC No. 1 s. 2017 and ensuring that the ECCD PPAs be incorporated as mandated under RA No. 10410.
observations.reason: ECCD program was not allocated in the SEF Budget since there were already fundings from the LCPC and GAD.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of complete BRS, prepare subsidiary ledgers per bank accounts, review the records relevant to the Cash in Bank account and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the financial statements and other documents; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to update religiously the cash books and the data reflected be preferably considered in the preparation of complete BRS to ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted.

We recommended that Management:
Make utmost effort to achieve a more in depth, complete physical count of all PPEs of the Municipality and submit the required RPCPPE within the prescribed period; and
Require both offices to reconcile property records with the accounting records and adjust the books of accounts and the property records, as warranted, after reconciliation.
For missing PPE items which can no longer be found/located despite great effort, we recommended that Management strictly follow the guidelines of COA Circular 2020-006 for the disposition of non-existing/missing PPE items of government agencies.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Demand for the settlement from all the accountable officers with existing unremitted collections and accountabilities;
Require the collecting officers to turn-over all future collections intact to the Municipal Treasurer and not to retain portion of them pursuant to Sections 8 and 26 of COA Circular No. 92-382; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to closely supervise the collecting officers and monitor their daily collections and remittances and require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for each of the collecting officers.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to analyze the affected accounts to determine which funds the amounts are due and prepare necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balances of each account in their respective fund classification.
Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct periodic reconciliation/analysis, preferably on a quarterly basis, of the reciprocal accounts “Due from/to Other Funds” to ensure that necessary adjustments in the books of accounts are made on time.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all end-user units of the Agency to prepare the PPMP, incorporating therein all their procurement requirements for supplies, materials and PPE during the year. This must be duly evaluated and reviewed by the Municipal Budget Officer and be submitted to the BAC Secretariat for consolidation into a comprehensive, workable and realistic APP for approval by the HoPE as basis for procurement.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all future procurements for supplies, materials and PPEs be supported by an approved PPMP/APP, pursuant to the appropriate documentary requirements provided in COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe/comply with the timeline for the submission of Financial Reports especially the Subsidiary Ledgers.
Devise Accounting procedures/methods to cope up with the submission of Accounting Reports to COA as well as other Financial Reports required by other agencies. Constantly remind/coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer and other concerned Offices within the LGU to submit the pending reports/documents in order to avoid delay in the preparation and submission of financial reports to COA.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned Municipal officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of purchase orders and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review. We also recommended that the concerned employees revisit the provisions of the IRR of the Procurement Law, specifically in employing different alternative modes of procurement, to ensure its compliance at all times.
We also directed the attention of Management to Section 4.0, the Penalty Clause, of COA Circular No. 2009-001, which states that the inability of the officials and employees concerned to comply with the requirements of the same must be subject to administrative disciplinary action and the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents may be suspended in audit.

We recommended that Management prioritize the preparation/ processing of documentary requirements to secure the TCT and other supporting documents of the purchased lots to support the claims of their ownership. The lands recorded in the books which were not supported with TCT and other legal documents of ownership must be properly disclosed in the notes to financial statements.
We also recommended that Management strictly comply with the documentary requirements to support the payments of the purchased lots as prescribed under PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Ensure that all disbursements made by the Municipality are supported with complete documentation as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
Ensure that the attached documents, such as the purchase orders, PRs, IARs, etc. are completely and properly signed and accomplished in compliance to the applicable laws and regulations to ascertain the validity and propriety of every transaction made by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer ensure that the MDRRMO have sufficient budget for MOOE such as office supplies expenses, TV subscription expenses and electricity expenses to avoid charging of the same against LDRRMF.
We also recommended that the MDRRM Officer reprogram the LDRRMFIP for CY 2022, and that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the proper charging of expenditures to the 70 per cent Mitigation Fund to ensure that the funds are utilized in accordance with its intended purpose.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to formulate a three-year program for strategic allocation of the SEF for public schools’ needs, taking into consideration the inclusion of the goals and objectives of the LGU’s CDP and LDIP, prior to the formulation and preparation of its Annual Budget.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive and all the members of the LSB ensure that the programs and activities for ECCD be incorporated in the budget for SEF. Additionally, the provisions of DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 in the utilization of the SEF for its intended purpose must be strictly observed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to establish and maintain complete GAD database to provide accurate information and bases for an effective gender planning, programming and policy formulation and utilized the GAD Fund only for acceptable PPAs that would address the identified gender issues in the locality based on RA No. 9710 MCW, PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-001 and other relevant international commitments.
We also recommended Management to consider attribution of a portion or the whole of the Agency’s major programs to the GAD Budget. As provided in PCW Memorandum Circular 2020-05, agencies are advised to use the tool for budget attribution found in Section 1.2.2.2 of the same Circular.

observations.managementAction:
The Agency promised to review the inventory listing in order to consider those PPEs that can no longer be found/located following the guidelines prescribed by COA.

Creation of GSO position is still on-going.
observations.reason: Partial physical count was conducted during early 2022, however, reconciliation between the accounting and property records was not yet done due to missing PPE items which can no longer be found despite great effort.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
	
Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the preparation and submission of the required BRS, prepare subsidiary ledgers per bank accounts, review the records relevant to the Cash in Bank account and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the financial statements and other documents; and

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to update religiously the cash books and the data reflected be preferably considered in the preparation of the BRS to ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted.
observations.managementAction: The Agency proposed to consider 2019 as the starting point of conducting complete bank reconciliation in order to speed up the process.
observations.reason: No BRS has been prepared by the Agency since 2011.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

a. Demand for the settlement from all the accountable officers with existing unremitted collections and accountabilities;
b. Require the collecting officers to turn-over all future collections intact to the Municipal Treasurer and not to retain portion of them pursuant to Sections 8 and 26 of COA Circular No. 92-382; and
c. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to closely supervise the collecting officers and monitor their daily collections and remittances and require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for each of the collecting officers.
observations.managementAction:
Demand letters were already issued to the collecting officers with unremitted balances. The Municipal Treasurer prepared ledgers to closely monitor the collections and remittances. Also, daily turn-over of collections are enforced.

The Municipal Treasurer is already monitoring the daily collections and remittances of the collecting officers and prohibits them to retain big amounts of collections.
observations.reason:
Enforcing the collecting officers to remit their unremitted balances is still on-going.

Reconciliation of balances between Accounting and Treasury Office is still on-going.

The collecting officers used to retain big amounts of money and are still in the process of remitting them to the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive strive to further improve the process relative to RPT and SET collections through:

Requiring the Municipal Assessor, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to follow the proper process in recording RPT and SET Receivables pursuant to the provisions prescribed under Section 248 of RA No. 7160 and Section 20, Volume I of NGAS for LGUs;

Posting of Notice of Delinquency in the payment of RPT at the Municipal Hall and in other public/conspicuous places all over the Municipality, in accordance with Section 254 of RA No. 7160; and

Requiring the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers pertaining to RPT and SET Receivables.
observations.reason:
Incomplete documents

RPTAR is not yet completely updated

Incomplete listing

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to analyze the affected accounts to determine which funds the amounts are due and prepare necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct balances of each account in their respective fund classification.

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct periodic reconciliation/analysis, preferably on a quarterly basis, of the reciprocal accounts “Due from/to Other Funds” to ensure that necessary adjustments in the books of accounts are made on time.
observations.managementAction: Out of the ₱301,038.26 unreconciled balance of Due from and Due to Other Funds as of December 31, 2021, only ₱65,000.00 remains as of March 2022. However, the Municipal Accountant is still having some difficulties in reconciling the remaining balance and keeping track of it since this pertains to prior years’ transactions.
observations.reason:
Balances in prior years can no longer be reconciled.

The Municipal Accountant could not conduct quarterly reconciliation due to voluminous transactions in the Accounting Office.

observations.managementAction:
Per analysis of the amounts in the accounting records, the Loans Payable account is overstated by an estimated amount of ₱412,000.00.

Since the LGU will be fully paid by May 2023, the excess amount in the books will be closed to the Government Equity.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Direct the Local Finance Committee to fast-track the approval of the revised/amended Municipal Revenue Code and formulate realistic income projections based on statistics of past years’ experiences and profile of local taxpayers and devise effective plan to avoid, mitigate and control the risks of possible non-collection of projected incomes; and

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to implement collection strategies to further improve revenue assessment and collection, such as:

Conducting and monitoring periodic collection performance to ensure efficient collection of projected income; and

Examining the books, accounts, and other pertinent records of any person, partnership, corporation, or association subject to local taxes, fees and charges in order to ascertain, assess, and collect the correct amount of the tax, fee, or charge.
observations.managementAction:
The Agency’s Local Revenue Code was already updated.

The Agency also devised collection strategies to improve revenue assessment and collection by:

a. Proposing computerized system for collections; and
b. Updating Revenue Code.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Agency transfer all unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to the STF at the end of every year in compliance with COA Circular 2012-002. In case where the actual cash transfer is not made, a transfer must still be recognized in the GF books with a corresponding trust liability in the TF books.

For CY 2020 unexpended balance of QRF and DRRM-MOOE of which cash transfer was only made in CY 2021, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize the transfer in the ensuing year as a Prior Period Adjustment in the GF books and a Trust Liability in the TF books, after reversing the entry for the Capital Outlay which must be made continuing in the GF until completion of the funded projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere to the provisions of Sections 114 and 121 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs on the proper recording and reporting of transactions, which requires that all regular purchases of supplies must be booked through the inventory account and the SSMI is needed as a basis for recording the expenditures using appropriate expenditure accounts;

Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to maintain the SLC and stock cards for each supplies and materials, to monitor and control the status of the stocks on hand; and

Consider the creation of a GSO position that will discharge on a full-time basis the procurement and issuance of supplies, materials and property; maintain stock cards per category on all inventories as recorded in the list of inventory accounting records; and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical count and preparation of the RPCI.
observations.managementAction:
Management promised to follow the prescribed rules and regulations once a storage room/house becomes available.

None

Creation of GSO position is still on-going.
observations.reason:
No designated/ available space for storage.

No designated/ available space for storage.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned Municipal officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission of POs and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review. We also recommended that the concerned employees revisit the provisions of the IRR of the Procurement Law, specifically in employing different alternative modes of procurement, to ensure its compliance at all times.

We also directed the attention of Management to Section 4.0, the Penalty Clause, of COA Circular No. 2009-001, which states that the inability of the officials and employees concerned to comply with the requirements of the same must be subject to administrative disciplinary action and the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents may be suspended in audit.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC required the end-users to prepare and submit the PRs at least ten working days before the actual activity to give BAC ample time to process and complete the documents ready for submission.

The Agency promised to comply with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason:
Lack of personnel and multiple suppliers, and delayed submission of PR by the end-users makes it difficult for the BAC to comply and submit on time.

Same reason in number 28.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the LGU officials and employees to submit promptly their claims together with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Municipal Accountant and direct the Municipal Accountant to fast-track the processing and submission of these vouchers to the Office of the Auditor;

Evaluate the process of preparing the disbursement vouchers and necessary documents to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must only be undertaken when they were properly approved and with complete documentation; and

Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445 and Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code, as amended by Section 41 of RA No. 10951, which may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render accounts without justifiable cause.
observations.managementAction: Monitoring was done, if not to stop the incurrence of delay in submission, but to at least shorten the number of days delayed.
observations.reason: Delays in the submission of accounts still recur.

observations.managementAction:
Only GSO-Designate was appointed and no office is still created to serve this purpose to control the movement of stocks.

Management promised to comply with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned personnel, particularly the authorized drivers of government vehicles, to prepare and submit regularly to the Office of the Auditor, within 10 days of the following month, the Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption and of Official Travels in accordance with government rules and regulations. 

We also recommended that all travel using government vehicle be controlled through the preparation of the accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets, which must be serially numbered. These accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets must be attached to the disbursement vouchers for the payment of fuel, oil and lubricants. Furthermore, it is then imperative that the Agency must comply with the rules and regulations on fuel consumption and keep its records in detail, as may be necessary, so that correct and accurate information will be provided.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all end-user units of the Agency to prepare the PPMP, incorporating therein all their procurement requirements for all supplies, materials and PPE during the year. This must be duly evaluated and reviewed by the Municipal Budget Officer and be submitted to the BAC Secretariat for consolidation into a comprehensive, workable and realistic APP for approval by the HoPE as basis for procurement.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all future procurements for supplies, materials and PPEs be supported by an approved PPMP/APP, pursuant to the appropriate documentary requirements provided in COA Circular 2012-001.
observations.managementAction:
The concerned agency officials were not yet aware that PPEs such as infrastructure projects shall also be incorporated in the APP.

The BAC promised to require the end-users to submit an approved PPMP and APP to the BAC before processing of any documents particularly the infrastructure projects and PPE items.

The BAC Secretariat also assured to include the infrastructure projects and other PPEs on the Consolidated APP.
observations.reason:
The concerned agency officials were not yet aware that PPEs such as infrastructure projects shall also be incorporated in the APP.

Same reason in number 41.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management submit to the Office of the Auditor the detailed plans or activity design for the distribution of the relief goods, the properly signed AIRs and DRs relevant to the procured items enumerated above, and the unsubmitted RDS. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

Ensure that all disbursements made by the Municipality are supported with complete documentation as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 2009-001; and 

Ensure that the attached documents, such as the purchase orders, purchase orders, IARs, etc. were properly signed and accomplished in compliance to the applicable laws and regulations to ascertain the validity and propriety of every transaction made by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Required documents were already submitted. Strict implementation is imposed for the succeeding transactions with the same nature as this to avoid recurrence of AOMs like this.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management establish and maintain GAD Database to provide accurate information and bases for an effective gender planning, programming and policy formulation, pursuant to Section 4.1.B.1 of JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA. We also recommended that the provisions of the afore-cited JMC relevant to the required appropriation for GAD PPAs of at least 5 per cent of the LGU’s total budget be properly observed, consistent with the total GAD appropriations per GPB; mainstreaming or supplemental budget can be resorted, if applicable, to achieve the minimum GAD appropriation for the year.
observations.managementAction: The concerned agency officials promised to follow the prescribed guidelines provided by the PCW.
observations.reason: The Municipality started to accumulate data for the establishment of a complete GAD Database.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct all department heads concerned to exert coordinated efforts in order to come up with an updated RPCPPE. 

We further recommended that all LGU officials/employees having direct concerns over these unrecorded properties be directed to retrieve the necessary documents and submit the same to the Property Officer and Accounting Unit for proper recording/accounting in the Agency’s books and that similar transactions in the future be duly accounted for upon acceptance.

Likewise, apply the necessary depreciation costs where warranted.
observations.managementAction:
The LGU is already in the process of retrieving the necessary documents.

On-going process

The Agency promised to review the inventory listing in order to consider those PPE’s that could no longer be found/located following the guidelines prescribed by COA.

Application of the necessary depreciation costs is on-going.
observations.reason: Missing PPEs during the conduct of inventory taking

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that all the Accountable Officers concerned be required to liquidate their cash advances on time so that proper accounting could be made thereof to ensure fairness of accounts presentation on the financial statements.

The Municipal Accountant in her capacity as the internal auditor must strictly implement the rules and regulations on cash advances to avoid the accumulation of huge amount of unliquidated cash advances.

Lastly, refrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant started to conduct reconciliation of the balances.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that Management require both the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to promptly cause the timely remittance of liabilities due to the national and corporate government agencies in order not to compromise the grant of benefits to the LGU’s officials and staff and at the same time avoid penalties/surcharges arising from late payments and to warrant timely use of these resources by the government agencies concerned.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management warrant the timely submission of documents as required by the memorandum circular so as to ensure the proper and effective implementation of the programs charged under the LCPC.
observations.managementAction: The MSWDO will submit this CY 2022 all the needed documents to the Office of the Auditor on time as prescribed by the provisions.
observations.reason: Delayed submission

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality submit a duly approved 10-year SWMP in order to properly address the need to ensure sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to exert more efforts to fast-track the revision and approval of the SWMP.
observations.reason: Some of the provisions of the SWMP are for revisions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GFPS fast-track the establishment of a GAD database to provide accurate information and bases for an effective gender planning, programming and policy formulation pursuant to Section 4.1.B.1 of JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.
observations.managementAction: Management will fast-track its completion and will be used for an effective GAD PPAs implementation.
observations.reason: GAD database is still incomplete.

Likewise, apply the necessary depreciation costs where warranted.

Same reason in number 53.

We recommended that all the accountable officer concerned be required to liquidate their cash advances on time so that proper accounting could be made thereof to ensure fairness of accounts presentation on the financial statements.

The Municipal Accountant in her capacity as the internal auditor must strictly implement the rules and regulations on cash advances to avoid the accumulation of huge amount of unliquidated cash advances.

Lastly, restrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that all concerned be required to observe full compliance to the aforecited rules and regulations to warrant timely rectification of defects and thereby reducing the risks of government funds wastage.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to submit the purchase orders and contracts together with all the supporting documents within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality submit a duly approved 10-year SWMP in order to properly address the need to ensure sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to exert more efforts to fast-track the revision and approval of the SWMP.
observations.reason: Some of the provisions of the SWMP are for revisions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exert its best effort to implement all programmed projects under the 20 per cent development fund during the year to ensure that its objectives of desirable socio-economic and environmental outcomes will be achieved.

We recommended that Management, initiate positive steps towards generation of increased income from local sources to warrant delivery of additional developmental projects in the locality, particularly on the collection of building permits. Also, we request that a breakdown on the agency’s approved annual budget as to sources of income be done.

The Agency proposed a computerized system.

Updating of Real Property Account Register is on-going.

Unsystematized collection.

On-going update of Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Require that an actual inventory-taking of all assets or PPEs of the LGU be
conducted each year pursuant to Section 124, Volume I of NGAS Manual for
LGUs; that the relevant RPCPPE be submitted not later than January 31 of
each year to the Office of the Auditor; and that the same be reconciled with
the records of the Municipal Accountant;
b. Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to maintain
Property Cards and PPELC, respectively, for all the Agency’s PPEs;
c. Consider creating a GSO who will discharge on a full-time basis the
procurement and issuance of supplies and property, maintain updated
Property Cards per category on all Agency’s assets as recorded in the list of
PPEs of accounting records, and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical
count and preparation of the RPCPPE; and
d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting entries to transfer the
PPEs enumerated above to their appropriate accounts and remind her to
carefully analyze every transaction before recording them in the Agency’s
books.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to give preferential attention on the
preparation and submission of the required BRS, prepare subsidiary ledgers
per bank accounts, review the records relevant to the Cash in Bank account
and effect necessary adjustments to correct the amount presented in the
financial statements and other documents; and
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to update religiously the cash books and the
data reflected be preferably considered in the preparation of the BRS to
ensure that any reconciling items will be accordingly adjusted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the collecting officers, by issuing a demand letter, to remit the
unremitted collections amounting in total to ₱1.16 million and to turn-over
all future collections intact to the Municipal Treasurer and not to retain
portion of them pursuant to Sections 8 and 26 of COA Circular No. 92-382;
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to closely supervise the collecting officers and
monitor their daily collections and remittances and require the Municipal
Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for each of the collecting officers;
c. Issue demand letters on the unremitted collections of the two former
Municipal Treasurers amounting in total to ₱2.10 million; and
d. Strengthen internal control over collections and remittances to avoid retention
of big amounts of money in the hands of the collecting officers and consider
the promulgation and implementation of a policy on the following:
i. On the turnover of daily collections, such as establishing a cut-off on the
time of turnover which will give the Municipal Treasurer enough time to
deposit the turned-over collections; and
ii. On imposing a penalty for holding of collections for several days in the
hands of collecting officers.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive strive to further improve the
process relative to RPT and SET collections through:
a. Requiring the Municipal Assessor, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal
Accountant to follow the proper process in recording RPT and SET
Receivables pursuant to the provisions prescribed under Section 248 of RA
No. 7160 and Section 20, Volume I of NGAS for LGUs;
b. Posting of Notice of Delinquency in the payment of RPT at the Municipal Hall
and in other public/conspicuous places all over the Municipality, in
accordance with Section 254 of RA No. 7160; and
c. Requiring the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain subsidiary
ledgers pertaining to RPT and SET Receivables.

We recommended that Management exert extra efforts to conduct verification,
analysis and validation of these receivable balances. We also recommended that
Management submit again a request letter to write-off the receivable accounts that
are dormant and uncollectible, following the procedures provided in COA Circular
No. 2016-005, and supported by the documents enumerated in Section 8.3 of the same
circular.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Analyze the affected accounts to determine to which funds the amounts are
due and prepare necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the correct
balances of each account in their respective fund classification; and
b. Conduct periodic reconciliation/analysis, preferably on a quarterly basis, of
the reciprocal accounts “Due from/Due to Other Funds” to ensure that
necessary adjustments in the books of accounts are made on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Make representations with the DBP for reconciliation of their records and
obtain copies of debit memos and other related loan documents as reference in
adjusting the books to reflect the correct balances of accounts in the financial
statements;
b. Strengthen accounting controls over loans payable by reviewing thoroughly
the amortization schedule given by the bank and carefully analyzing
transaction before recording in the books and updating entries of both
records;
c. Reclassify the current portion of the Loans Payable – Domestic account
amounting to ₱4.56 million to its proper classification in the Statement of
Financial Position; and
d. Disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the details of the Loans Payable
– Domestic pursuant to Paragraph 127 of IPSAS No. 1.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Local Finance Committee to fast-track the approval of the
revised/amended Municipal Revenue Code and formulate realistic income
projections based on statistics of past years’ experiences and profile of local
taxpayers and devise effective plan to avoid, mitigate and control the risks of
possible non-collection of projected incomes; and
b. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to implement collection strategies to further
improve revenue assessment and collection, such as:
i. Conducting and monitoring periodic collection performance to ensure
efficient collection of projected income; and
ii. Examining the books, accounts, and other pertinent records of any
person, partnership, corporation, or association subject to local taxes, fees
and charges in order to ascertain, assess, and collect the correct amount of
the tax, fee, or charge.

We recommended that the Agency transfer all unexpended/unobligated
balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to the STF at the end of every
year in compliance with COA Circular 2012-002. In case where the actual cash
transfer is not made, a transfer must still be recognized in the GF books with a
corresponding trust liability in the TF books.
For CY 2020 unexpended balance of QRF and DRRM-MOOE of which cash
transfer was only made in CY 2021, we recommended that the Municipal
Accountant recognize the transfer in the ensuing year as a Prior Period
Adjustment in the GF books and a Trust Liability in the TF books, after reversing
the entry for the Capital Outlay which must be made continuing in the GF until
completion of the funded projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere
to the provisions of Sections 114 and 121 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs on
the proper recording and reporting of transactions, which requires that all
regular purchases of supplies must be booked through the inventory account
and the SSMI is needed as a basis for recording the expenditures using
appropriate expenditure accounts;
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to maintain the
SLC and stock cards for each supplies and materials, to monitor and control
the status of the stocks on hand; and
c. Consider the creation of a GSO position that will discharge on a full-time basis
the procurement and issuance of supplies, materials and property; maintain
stock cards per category on all inventories as recorded in the list of inventory
accounting records; and facilitate the proper conduct of the physical count
and preparation of the RPCI.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned
Municipal officials and employees to strictly comply with the mandatory submission
of POs and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days
after perfection for a timely and complete conduct of auditorial and technical review.
We also recommended that the concerned employees revisit the provisions of the IRR
of the Procurement Law, specifically in employing different alternative modes of
procurement, to ensure its compliance at all times.
We also directed the attention of Management to Section 4.0, the Penalty
Clause, of COA Circular No. 2009-001, which states that the inability of the officials
and employees concerned to comply with the requirements of the same must be
subject to administrative disciplinary action and the transactions covered by the
unsubmitted documents may be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Require the LGU officials and employees to submit promptly their claims
together with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Municipal
Accountant and direct the Municipal Accountant to fast-track the processing
and submission of these vouchers to the Office of the Auditor;
b. Evaluate the process of preparing the disbursement vouchers and necessary
documents to prevent further occurrence of this deficiency in the succeeding
years and be reminded that processing of vouchers must only be undertaken
when they were properly approved and with complete documentation; and
c. Remind the concerned officials and employees of the administrative or
criminal liability, under Section 39 (1) (3) of PD No. 1445 and Article 218 of
the Revised Penal Code, as amended by Section 41 of RA No. 10951, which
may be imposed as a consequence of their inability or refusal to render
accounts without justifiable cause.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer, as the Property/Supply Officer, and the
Municipal Accountant to monitor the movement of stocks thru the use and
maintenance of RIS and stock cards/stock ledger cards;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required documentary
requirements supporting all purchases especially for drugs and medicines are
present;
c. Direct the requesting officers on the procurement of medicines to review the
provisions of EO No. 49, s. 1993 and ensure the Agency’s compliance on them,
as such, a certification signed by the requesting officer that the drug products
being requisitioned conform with PNDF Volume I is required to support the
procurement; and
d. Require the department heads to prepare (Project Procurement Management
Plans) PPMP, for the procurement of all supplies and materials that their
office/s will be needing within the CY, and submit them to the BAC for
consolidation. Be reminded that an APP must include all procurement
activities planned for the year, not just for office supplies, and only those
projects/procurement included therein will be undertaken within the year.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned
personnel, particularly the authorized drivers of government vehicles, to prepare and
submit regularly to the Office of the Auditor, within 10 days of the following month,
the Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption and of Official Travels in accordance with
government rules and regulations.
We also recommended that all travel using government vehicle be controlled
through the preparation of the accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets, which must be
serially numbered. These accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets must be attached to the
disbursement vouchers for the payment of fuel, oil and lubricants. Furthermore, it is
then imperative that the Agency must comply with the rules and regulations on fuel
consumption and keep its records in detail, as may be necessary, so that correct and
accurate information will be provided.

We recommended that Management prioritize the preparation/processing of
documentary requirements to secure the TCT and other supporting documents of the
purchased lots to support the claims of their ownership. The lands recorded in the
books which were not supported with TCT and other legal documents of ownership
must be properly disclosed in the notes to financial statements.
We recommended that Management strictly comply with the documentary
requirements to support the payments of the purchased lots as prescribed under PD
1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all end-user units of
the Agency to prepare the PPMP, incorporating therein all their procurement
requirements for all supplies, materials and PPE during the year. This must be duly
evaluated and reviewed by the Municipal Budget Officer and be submitted to the
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat for consolidation into a
comprehensive, workable and realistic APP for approval by the Head of Procuring
Entity (HoPE) as basis for procurement.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all future
procurements for supplies, materials and PPEs be supported by an approved
PPMP/APP, pursuant to the appropriate documentary requirements provided in
COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that Management:
a. Submit a satisfactory explanation as to the appropriateness and
reasonableness of the above-noted observations in the grant of financial
assistance;
b. Regulate the grant of financial assistance to the most essential only and ensure
that funds disbursed are property utilized for public welfare and refrain from
giving financial assistance which does not conform with the exigencies of the
service;
c. Provide specific guidelines or basis for the rates of the financial assistance
given to LGU employees and barangay officials and employees; and
d. Ensure that future claims be supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management submit to the Office of the Auditor the
detailed plans or activity design for the distribution of the relief goods, the properly
signed AIRs and DRs relevant to the procured items enumerated above, and the
unsubmitted RDS.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Ensure that all disbursements made by the Municipality are supported with
complete documentation as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 and
COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
b. Ensure that the attached documents, such as the POs, PRs, IARs, etc. were
properly signed and accomplished in compliance to the applicable laws and
regulations to ascertain the validity and propriety of every transaction made
by the Municipality.

We recommended that Management establish and maintain GAD Database to
provide accurate information and bases for an effective gender planning,
programming and policy formulation, pursuant to Section 4.1.B of the PCW, DILG,
DBM, and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01. We also recommended that the provisions of
the afore-cited JMC relevant to the required appropriation for GAD PPAs of at least 5
per cent of the LGU’s total budget be properly observed, consistent with the total
GAD appropriations per GPB; mainstreaming or supplemental budget can be
resorted, if applicable, to achieve the minimum GAD appropriation for the year.

1. We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct all department heads concerned to exert coordinated efforts in order to come up with an updated RPCPPE.
2. We further recommended that all LGU officials/employees having direct concerns over these unrecorded properties be directed to retrieve the necessary documents and submit the same to the Property Officer and Accounting Unit for proper recording/accounting in the Agency’s books and that similar transactions in the future be duly accounted for upon acceptance.
3. Likewise, apply the necessary depreciation costs where warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Honorable Mayor will issue a new directive in this regard.
observations.reason:
The Inventory Committee was not functional. Nonetheless, the Municipality was
Not able to conduct a partial physical count but unable to
prepare the required RPCPPE.

observations.recommendation:
4. We reiterated our recommendation that all the Accountable Officers concerned be required to liquidate their cash advances on time so that proper accounting could be made thereof to ensure fairness of accounts presentation on the financial statements.
5. The Municipal Accountant in her capacity as the internal auditor must strictly implement the rules and regulations on cash advances to avoid the accumulation of huge amount of unliquidated cash advances.
6. Lastly, refrain from granting cash advances to AOs, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled.
observations.managementAction: Management tends to issue demand letters to the officials and employees with unliquidated or unsettled cash advances.
observations.reason: Some are still not liquidated; however, the Municipality does not grant additional unliquidated cash advances to those with unliquidated or unsettled cash advances.

observations.recommendation: 7. We reiterated our recommendation that Management require both the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to promptly cause the timely remittance of liabilities due to the national and corporate government agencies in order not to compromise the grant of benefits to the LGU’s officials and staff and at the same time avoid penalties/surcharges arising from late payments and to warrant timely use of these resources by the government agencies concerned.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer strive to comply with the recommendations and remit the statutory Municipal contributions as soon as possible.
observations.reason: Some remittances were still delayed due to the voluminous transactions that preoccupied the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: 8. We reiterated our recommendation that Management warrant the timely submission of documents as required by the memorandum circular so as to ensure the proper and effective implementation of the programs charged under the LCPC.
observations.managementAction: The MSWDO will submit this CY 2021 all the needed documents to the Office of the Auditor on time as prescribed by the provisions.
observations.reason: Delayed submission

observations.recommendation:
12. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all department heads concerned to exert coordinated efforts in order to come up with an updated RPCPPE.
13. We further recommended that all Local Government Unit (LGU) officials/employees having direct concerns over these unbooked properties be directed to retrieve the necessary documents and submit the same to the Property Officer and Accounting Unit for proper recording/accounting in the Agency’s books and that similar transactions in the future be duly accounted for upon acceptance.
14. Likewise, apply the necessary depreciation costs where warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Honorable Mayor will issue a new directive in this regard.
observations.reason: Not able to conduct a partial physical count but unable to prepare the required RPCPPE.

observations.recommendation:
15. We recommended that all the accountable officers concerned be required to liquidate their cash advances on time so that proper accounting could be made thereof to ensure fairness of accounts presentation on the financial statements.
16. The Municipal Accountant in her capacity as the internal auditor must strictly implement the rules and regulations on cash advances to avoid the accumulation of huge amount of unliquidated cash advances.
17. Lastly, restrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled.
observations.managementAction: Management tends to issue demand letters to the officials and employees with unliquidated or unsettled cash advances.
observations.reason: Some are still not liquidated; the Municipality does not grant additional unliquidated cash advances to those with unliquidated or unsettled cash advances.

observations.recommendation: 18. We recommended that all concerned be required to observe full compliance to the aforecited rules and regulations to warrant timely rectification of defects and thereby reducing the risks of government funds wastage.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to submit the POs and contracts together with all the supporting documents within the prescribed period.
observations.reason: Delayed submission and incomplete attachments.

observations.recommendation: 9 / 19. We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipality submit a duly approved 10-year SWMP in order to properly address the need to ensure sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to fast-track the revision and approval of the SWMP.
observations.reason: Some of the provisions of the SWMP are for fast-track revisions.

observations.recommendation: 20. We recommended that Management exert its best effort to implement all programmed projects under the 20 per cent development fund during the year to ensure that its objectives of desirable socio-economic and environmental outcomes will be achieved.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to exert its best effort to implement the programmed projects.

observations.recommendation: 21. We recommended that Management, initiate positive steps towards generation of increased income from local sources to warrant delivery of additional developmental projects in the locality, particularly on the collection of building permits. Also, we request that a breakdown on the agency’s approved annual budget as to sources of income be done.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Tax Code is on its 2nd reading. Management promised its faster approval and implementation.
observations.reason: The provisions in the Municipal Revenue Code were not fully implemented. Also, the Municipal Tax Code is on its 2nd reading.

observations.recommendation:
22. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all department heads concerned to exert coordinated efforts in order to come up with an updated Report of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment as of December 31, 2018.
23. It is further recommended that all LGU officials/employees having direct concerns over these properties be directed to retrieve the necessary documents and submit the same to the Property Officer and Accounting Unit for proper recording in the agency’s books and that similar transactions in the future be accounted for upon acceptance.
observations.managementAction: The Honorable Mayor will issue a new directive in this regard.
observations.reason: Initiated and started but not completed.

observations.recommendation:
24. We recommended that all department heads concerned be required to observe full compliance to the aforecited rules and regulations to warrant timely rectification of defects and thereby reduce the risks of government funds wastage and to aid the LGU Senior Management in its decision making in future transaction.
25. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to require copy of request for final inspection, duly received by the COA Audit Team, in all claims for final payments for completed infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to submit the POs and contracts together with all the supporting documents within the prescribed period.
observations.reason: Delayed submission and incomplete attachments.

observations.recommendation:
26. We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact and regularly all collections turned over to him to the agency’s authorized depository bank pursuant to the foregoing regulations; and
27. Strictly monitor the collections and turn-over activities of all accountable officers and see to it that the laws, rules and regulations governing collections and deposits are strictly complied with to ensure that the agency’s cash resources are adequately safeguarded.
observations.managementAction: The accountable officers’ practice to retain some of the daily collections and the undeposited collections of the previous treasurers. Strategies were being implemented in the Office and demand letters will be sent to the previous treasurers.
observations.reason: The accountable officers’ practice to retain some of the daily collections and the undeposited balances existed; strategies were being implemented and demand letters will be sent to the previous treasurers.

observations.recommendation:
28. Implement immediately the PPA’s as embodied in the approved Annual Development Plan in order to optimize the delivery of basic services to the people; and
29. Require the Local Development Council to include in their AIPs development projects which are feasible and to assure its implementation as to target dates.
observations.managementAction: Management implemented the recommended actions.

Likewise, apply the necessary depreciation costs where warranted.

observations.managementAction: Partial Inventory Reports per department; attached with updated ARE’s were submitted pursuant to the directive of the Honorable Mayor.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer started to secure the individual inventory report and properties found at the different offices. Unfortunately, no RCPPE for the year were submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the senior LGU management strictly comply with and adhere to the provisions governing grant and liquidation of cash advances and in order to prevent unnecessary accumulation of cash on hand thereby mitigating the risk of fund misuse/misappropriation.
observations.managementAction: Closer supervision over this activity is being undertaken.
observations.reason: The Honorable Mayor issued a directive in relation to our recommendation. Compliance thereto were noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

a. Maintain updated Property Cards per category on all property, plant, and equipment as recorded in the list of Property, Plant, and Equipment of accounting records, and thereafter as the transaction occurs.

b. Reconcile property records ensuring that the balances shown in the Prepared RCPPE agreed with the supply records and the book balances in the accounting records, and vice versa.

c. Maintain and renew all Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment every three years for the signature of the holder of each item listed in the prepared RCPPE or based on the old Memorandum Receipts on File.

d. Submit the copy of the Invoice Receipt showing the certification of the Outgoing Official pertaining to the returned/turned over properties and acknowledgment receipt of the Incoming Official/s of the property surrendered by the Outgoing Official including the signatures of the witnesses for the turn-over.
observations.managementAction: Updated ARE’s are now submitted.
observations.reason: ARE’s are now being issued

observations.recommendation:
We recommended full compliance by LGU-Pio V. Corpus to the provisions of Republic Act No. 9003, particularly to the role of the LGU in the nationwide campaign for an effective ecological solid waste management program.

Install an effective collection and disposal of solid wastes, giving emphasis to its segregation and proper disposal.
observations.managementAction: The LGU is continuously looking for an area for sanitary landfill that will meet the requirement of the DENR.
observations.reason: While we noted intensified garbage collection. No sanitary landfill was provided.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated agency’s compliance to the audit recommendations offered in the previous audit reports which primarily dealt on the assurance of submitting updated RPCPPE and the regular conduct of physical inventory taking.
observations.managementAction: (See related CY 2016 audit observation no. 1)
observations.reason: (See related CY 2016 audit observation no. 1)

observations.managementAction: Both the BAC Chairperson and the Secretariat were ordered full compliance hereto.
observations.reason: Although submission of the required documents were made, they were not done in the prescribed period.
